Innovations in Seawater Desalination Technologies: Shaping the Future of Water Supply
=====================================================================================

[Drinking water and sanitation](https://krakensense.com/blog/category/Drinking+water+and+sanitation)[Technology](https://krakensense.com/blog/category/Technology)

Jul 30

Written By [Jade Hall](https://krakensense.com/blog?author=62d577ce7a637605a2fd4c22)

Water scarcity is an escalating global crisis, driven by a combination of population growth, climate change, and over-exploitation of freshwater resources. Traditional freshwater sources, such as rivers, lakes, and groundwater, are under immense stress, with many regions experiencing severe shortages. As the demand for potable water continues to rise, the limitations of current water supply systems become increasingly evident.

The global water crisis demands innovative solutions to ensure a sustainable and reliable freshwater supply. Over the past decade, seawater desalination has experienced accelerated growth, largely driven by significant advances in membrane technology and material science. This growth has not only improved the efficiency of desalination processes but also reduced the cost of producing fresh water from saline sources, including seawater, brackish water, and treated wastewater.

![Image 3](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/629780ad60b2ca4521d4fcbe/f23156ae-e72f-4d12-a75b-7bfc2ee619c0/photos+%2815%29.png)

What is Desalination?
---------------------

Desalination is the process of removing salts and other impurities from seawater or brackish water to produce fresh, potable water suitable for human consumption, agriculture, and industrial use. As freshwater resources become increasingly scarce due to population growth, climate change, and over-extraction, desalination provides a vital alternative source of water. The oceans, which cover more than 70% of the Earth's surface, provide an almost limitless supply of water. As a result, desalination can provide a reliable and continuous supply of fresh water, especially for arid and semi-arid regions where natural freshwater resources are scarce or depleted.

Additionally, desalination helps reduce the pressure on over-exploited freshwater resources. Groundwater depletion and the drying up of rivers and lakes are critical issues in many parts of the world. By supplementing traditional water sources with desalinated water, communities can reduce their dependence on these stressed resources, allowing them to recover and ensuring their sustainability for future generations.

However, existing desalination technologies are often criticized for their high energy consumption and environmental impact, underscoring the urgent need for innovative advancements in this field. Conventional desalination methods, such as reverse osmosis (RO), are energy-intensive, making them costly and environmentally taxing due to their reliance on fossil fuels. Furthermore, these processes produce large volumes of brine, a concentrated salt solution that can harm marine ecosystems if not properly managed.

Types of Desalination Processes
-------------------------------

#### Reverse Osmosis

Reverse Osmosis (RO) is currently the most widely used desalination technology, accounting for approximately [60% of global desalinated water production](https://www.preventionweb.net/news/desalination-may-be-key-averting-global-water-shortage-it-will-take-time). In the RO process, seawater is forced through semi-permeable membranes under high pressure. These membranes allow water molecules to pass while blocking salts and other impurities. The result is clean, fresh water on one side of the membrane and concentrated brine on the other.

RO systems typically involve multiple stages to ensure thorough purification. Initially, water undergoes pre-filtration to remove larger particles like sediment and chlorine, which could damage the RO membrane. Once pre-filtered, the water is pressurized and forced through the semi-permeable membrane. This membrane selectively allows water molecules to pass through, while contaminants are trapped and flushed away as waste. Post-filtration may follow to further polish the water and eliminate any residual taste or odor, ensuring the highest possible water quality.

One of the significant advantages of RO is its capability to effectively remove a wide range of contaminants, including dissolved solids, heavy metals, pathogens, and organic compounds. This ability not only enhances the taste and odor of water but also ensures its safety for consumption and various industrial applications. Moreover, RO systems are adaptable and scalable, ranging from small-scale household units to large-scale industrial installations, catering to diverse water purification needs.

Despite its advantages, RO systems come with certain challenges and considerations. The process typically generates a significant amount of wastewater, known as brine, which contains concentrated contaminants and requires proper disposal or treatment. Additionally, the operation of RO systems can be energy-intensive due to the high-pressure pumps needed to drive water through the membrane. Regular maintenance, including membrane cleaning and replacement, is also necessary to sustain optimal system performance and longevity.

#### Thermal Desalination

Thermal desalination is a process that utilizes heat to separate freshwater from saline wate. Unlike reverse osmosis (RO), which relies on membranes and pressure to filter out salts, thermal desalination operates on the principle of evaporation and condensation.

There are two main types of thermal desalination processes: Multi-Stage Flash Distillation (MSF) and Multiple-Effect Distillation (MED). In MSF, seawater is heated under high pressure in multiple stages, each at progressively lower pressures. As the seawater enters each stage, it flashes into vapor due to the pressure drop, and this vapor is then condensed to produce fresh water. The remaining concentrated brine is discharged, typically back into the ocean.

On the other hand, MED uses a series of evaporator vessels known as "effects." Each effect operates at a progressively lower pressure, allowing the water to evaporate and condense multiple times as it moves through the system. The heat from the condensing vapor in each effect is used to evaporate water in the next effect, maximizing energy efficiency. MED systems are known for their energy efficiency and are often used in conjunction with waste heat from power plants or industrial processes.

Thermal desalination offers several advantages. One significant benefit is its flexibility in energy sources. It can utilize various heat sources, including waste heat from industrial processes, solar thermal energy, or fossil fuels, depending on the availability and cost of energy in the region. This flexibility makes thermal desalination suitable for areas where traditional energy sources are abundant or where renewable energy options are preferred.

Another advantage of thermal desalination is its robustness against variations in feedwater quality. Unlike RO, which can be sensitive to the quality of the feedwater, thermal desalination processes can handle water with higher levels of impurities without compromising efficiency. This makes it particularly useful in treating brackish water or seawater with varying levels of salinity and contaminants.

However, thermal desalination requires significant amounts of heat to evaporate water, which can result in higher operational costs, especially if fossil fuels are used for heating. Moreover, the disposal of concentrated brine can pose environmental challenges. The high salt content and temperature of the brine can harm marine ecosystems if not properly managed, requiring careful consideration of discharge practices.

Innovations in Desalination
---------------------------

#### Reverse Osmosis Enhancements

Recent advancements in reverse osmosis technology have significantly enhanced the efficiency and sustainability of desalination processes, making them more viable solutions for addressing global water scarcity challenges. One of the key innovations in RO technology is the development of graphene oxide membranes. These membranes represent a leap forward from traditional polymer-based membranes by offering superior filtration capabilities. Graphene oxide membranes have higher permeability, which means they allow more water molecules to pass through while effectively blocking salts and other contaminants. This increased permeability not only improves the overall efficiency of RO systems but also reduces energy consumption per cubic meter of freshwater produced.

Energy recovery devices (ERDs) have also played a crucial role in advancing RO technology. ERDs are integrated into modern desalination plants to capture and reuse energy from the concentrated brine (waste stream). During the RO process, high-pressure brine is discharged as a byproduct. ERDs utilize this pressure to pressurize incoming seawater before it enters the membranes. By recovering energy that would otherwise be wasted, ERDs significantly reduce the amount of external energy needed to operate desalination plants. This innovation has led to substantial improvements in energy efficiency and operational costs, making RO more economically viable and environmentally sustainable.

Furthermore, advancements in membrane vessel configurations and system design have contributed to the efficiency gains in RO technology. Innovations such as spiral-wound membrane modules and improved flow dynamics within the vessels have optimized the desalination process. These enhancements not only increase the durability and longevity of membranes but also improve overall system performance, leading to higher water recovery rates and reduced maintenance requirements.

#### Forward Osmosis

Forward Osmosis (FO) is an emerging technology that uses a natural osmotic pressure gradient to draw water through a semi-permeable membrane, leaving salt and other impurities behind. In FO, water naturally flows from a feed solution (often seawater or brackish water) through a membrane into a draw solution, which has a higher osmotic pressure due to dissolved solutes such as salts.

One significant innovation in FO is the development of highly hydrophilic membranes. These membranes have a strong attraction to water molecules, which improves water flux— the rate at which water passes through the membrane— and reduces fouling. Fouling occurs when contaminants build up on the membrane surface, reducing its efficiency over time. By enhancing membrane hydrophilicity, FO systems can maintain high water flux rates and extend membrane longevity, making them more reliable and cost-effective for long-term operation.

Another notable advancement is the integration of hybrid systems combining FO with reverse osmosis or other desalination technologies. Hybrid FO-RO systems leverage the strengths of each method to optimize desalination processes. FO operates efficiently in low-pressure environments, making it suitable for pre-concentration of feedwater before it enters an RO system. This pre-concentration reduces the load on the RO membranes, leading to lower energy consumption and operational costs. By complementing FO's natural osmotic process with RO's high rejection capabilities, hybrid systems can achieve higher overall water recovery rates and produce high-quality freshwater efficiently.

#### Capacitive Deionization (CDI)

Capacitive Deionization (CDI) is a novel electrochemical process that removes salts from water by applying an electrical potential to electrodes. Unlike conventional desalination methods that rely on pressure or membranes, CDI operates through the principles of electrostatic attraction and adsorption.

In a typical CDI system, there are two electrodes usually made of porous carbon materials such as activated carbon or carbon nanotubes. These electrodes are placed in a cell configuration where they are separated by a space through which water flows. When a low voltage electrical potential is applied across the electrodes, ions in the water are attracted to the charged surface of the electrodes. The porous structure of the electrodes facilitates the adsorption of ions from the water, effectively reducing the concentration of salts and other dissolved contaminants.

One of the key advancements in CDI technology is the development of nanostructured electrodes. By utilizing nanostructured carbon materials such as carbon nanotubes and graphene, the surface area of electrodes is significantly increased. This larger surface area allows for more efficient ion adsorption and desorption processes. Nanostructured electrodes also enhance the electrical conductivity within the system, improving the overall performance and energy efficiency of CDI. These innovations have led to higher ion removal capacities per unit area of electrode, making CDI systems more effective and economical for treating saline water.

Another important innovation in CDI is the implementation of flow-through designs. Traditionally, CDI systems operate in a batch mode where water is circulated through the electrodes and ions are adsorbed onto the surface under an applied voltage. Flow-through CDI, however, allows water to pass directly through the electrode materials. This configuration enhances ion removal rates and reduces the energy consumption associated with pumping and recirculating water through the system. Flow-through CDI systems are particularly advantageous for continuous water treatment applications, offering improved efficiency and operational flexibility compared to conventional CDI setups.

#### Solar Desalination

Harnessing solar energy for desalination offers a sustainable and renewable solution, particularly for regions with abundant sunlight.

One of the primary methods of solar desalination is through solar stills, which have evolved significantly with technological innovations. Modern solar stills incorporate advanced materials and designs aimed at maximizing evaporation rates and water yield. Hydrophobic membranes and specialized solar absorbers are used to enhance solar energy absorption and transfer, facilitating more efficient evaporation of water from the saline source. These improvements not only increase the overall efficiency of solar stills but also reduce energy consumption compared to conventional heating methods.

Another innovative approach in solar desalination is the use of Photovoltaic-Thermal (PVT) systems. PVT systems integrate photovoltaic (PV) cells with thermal collectors in a single device. PV cells convert sunlight directly into electricity, while thermal collectors absorb solar energy to generate heat. This dual functionality allows PVT systems to produce both electricity and heat simultaneously, making them highly efficient for powering desalination processes. The generated electricity can directly power desalination units, while the waste heat from PV operation can be utilized to enhance water evaporation or distillation processes, further improving overall system efficiency.

The integration of PVT systems with desalination technologies offers several advantages. By utilizing renewable solar energy, PVT systems reduce reliance on fossil fuels and mitigate greenhouse gas emissions associated with traditional energy sources. They also provide a reliable and sustainable solution for decentralized water production in remote or off-grid locations, where access to freshwater is limited.

Solar desalination technologies continue to evolve with ongoing research and development efforts aimed at improving system efficiency, scalability, and cost-effectiveness. Innovations in materials, such as advanced membranes and selective coatings, are being explored to further enhance solar energy absorption and heat transfer in desalination processes. Additionally, advancements in system integration and control technologies are paving the way for optimized operation and maintenance of solar desalination plants.

#### Electrodialysis (ED)

Electrodialysis (ED) is a specialized desalination technology that utilizes an electric field to separate ions from water. In an ED system, alternating anion-selective and cation-selective membranes are arranged in a stack. When an electric potential is applied across the stack, positively charged ions (cations) migrate towards the negatively charged cathode through cation-selective membranes, while negatively charged ions (anions) move towards the positively charged anode through anion-selective membranes. This selective ion transport effectively separates salts and other dissolved substances from water, producing purified water and concentrated brine streams.

Recent advancements in ED technology have focused on improving membrane materials and enhancing system efficiency. One notable innovation is the development of bipolar membranes. These membranes have the unique capability to split water molecules into hydrogen (H+) and hydroxyl (OH-) ions when an electric current is applied. This process generates acid and base solutions within the ED system, which can be used to clean and regenerate the membranes periodically. By reducing fouling and scaling, bipolar membranes extend the operational lifespan of ED systems and lower maintenance requirements, making them more sustainable and cost-effective over time.

Energy efficiency is another key area of innovation in ED technology. Advances in stack design and operation have led to the development of asymmetric electrode configurations and improved ion-selective membranes. These enhancements minimize energy losses during ion transport through the membranes, thereby reducing the overall energy consumption of ED systems. By optimizing the electrical field distribution and ion flow dynamics within the stack, modern ED designs achieve higher salt removal efficiencies at lower operational costs compared to earlier generations of the technology.

The application of ED extends beyond traditional desalination to include various industrial processes and wastewater treatment applications. ED systems are utilized in industries such as food and beverage manufacturing, pharmaceuticals, and power generation, where precise control over ion concentrations and water quality is critical. Additionally, ED plays a role in environmental remediation by treating contaminated water sources and reducing the environmental footprint of industrial operations.

### What Does the Future Hold?

The continuous decline in desalinated water production costs, combined with rising water treatment costs due to stricter regulatory requirements, is expected to drive an increased reliance on desalination. Innovations in seawater desalination are setting the stage for a future where access to fresh water is more reliable and sustainable. As these technologies continue to evolve, desalination is poised to become a cornerstone of global water supply, ensuring that even the most water-scarce regions have access to this vital resource.

Abstract
--------

The emergence of new technologies offers many possibilities to expand the desalination sector, which has historically relied on thermal desalination (phase change) and membrane-based desalination (non-phase change). This study examines the latest developments in desalination technologies, namely those approaching commercial feasibility. It also investigates integrating renewable energy sources, such as forward osmosis (FO) and membrane distillation (MD), to power these systems. Furthermore, it examines the possibility of utilising electrochemical methods to reach technical maturity and scalability. In the last ten years, there have been notable advancements in desalination technology, namely reverse osmosis (RO) systems. These advancements have resulted in lower costs for desalinated water and a higher water demand. Water expenses can significantly change among various areas and establishments owing to variables such as subsidies and municipal incentives. Existing cost calculation tools have limitations and lack broad applicability. Implementing a comprehensive cost-estimating approach can assist in determining the best appropriate desalination technology for a given area. The reverse osmosis (RO) system driven by photovoltaics provided the most cost-effective production with electrical energy consumption of almost 2.kWh m−3 and cost of production of $0.26–1.33 m−3. This article advocates for more research to formulate solutions to reduce these expenses, effectively handling the disposal of brine wastes, and exploring renewable energy options as substitutes for non-renewable fuels.

Introduction
------------

The scarcity of freshwater resources has emerged as a prominent global concern owing to the pervasive prevalence of water contamination ([Bernauer & Böhmelt, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0070)). Common ways of acquiring freshwater include day-to-day living by treating wastewater, collecting water from the atmosphere, and desalinating seawater ([Cai et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0095), [Li et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0365), [Zhang et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0625)). Given the ample availability of salt water, seawater desalination (SD) is regarded as a desirable choice for freshwater production ([Kunjaram et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0340), [Wang et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0610)). SD can be categorised into two main methods: thermal distillation (TD) and membrane-based desalination (MBD) ([Huang et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0260), [Ji et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0285)). A primary obstacle in the desalination sector is the substantial expenses associated with establishing and functioning desalination plants, which directly impact the overall cost of the generated water. It is possible to optimise both the form and the dimensions of desalination systems to reduce energy costs by incorporating renewable energy sources (RES) ([Shokri and Sanavi Fard, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0555), [Shokri and Sanavi Fard, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0560)). Additionally, substantial developments in membrane and thermal systems have the potential to significantly reduce the expense of desalination structures, both in terms of their initial investment and their ongoing costs of operation. The economic feasibility of desalination technology is influenced by system lifespan and operating costs. Economic necessity drives demand for desalinated water, especially in water scarcity regions, regardless of water cost ([Ziolkowska, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0640)). Furthermore, due to the substantial uncertainty associated with the functioning and development of desalination facilities, as well as the unpredictable swings in meteorological conditions that cannot be accurately forecasted in advance, it is impractical to anticipate the long-term outcome of a recently built desalination plant. To maintain desalination's importance in the water portfolio, it's suggested to reduce water production costs to match traditional resources without shutting down facilities ([Ziolkowska, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0640)). While the cost of desalinated water might vary greatly, the efficiency of desalination systems can be greatly improved by considering the countries' geographical and economic situations in the regions where the plants are located.

The predominant focus of current research in desalination technology lies in developing novel materials and enhancing evaporation efficiency ([Anis et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0045)). Nonetheless, the advancement of this technology will be hindered if the real expenses and adverse ecological consequences are not considered ([Mezher et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0395)). The study on evaporation technology is superficial due to limited coverage, necessitating a deeper understanding of the pros and cons of various desalination technologies for efficient procedures.

Comparing desalination costs across nations is challenging due to environmental factors, diverse operating rules, socio-economic characteristics, and geographical situations, as well as a lack of specific data and standard cost calculation methods ([Lapuente, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0345)). It would be necessary to consider many factors to establish an accurate comparison, even between two different plants in the same region. Separate from operational expenses are capital and maintenance costs, which make up the total cost of desalination. The total cost of water desalination is illustrated in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#f0005). Capital costs encompass all expenses incurred during the lifecycle of a desalination plant, including but not limited to design, construction, finance, permits, acceptance testing for normal operation, pre- and post-commissioning, and design ([Panagopoulos, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0460)). Total water cost, often called life-cycle cost, is the sum of the costs of operations and investments for a specific period. A year's worth of desalination water volumes divided by the sum of all operating, capital, and maintenance costs yields a price. The fixed and variable components of operation and maintenance expenses make them site-specific.

Indirect costs (such as LEED design, engineering, supervision, and construction overheads) and direct expenditures (such as power, brine disposal systems, and process equipment) are examples of capital costs. The expenses of operations and maintenance are divided into two categories: variable costs (such as labour, energy, chemicals, and spare parts) and fixed costs (such as insurance and amortisation). This framework allows for a thorough analysis of the overall water cost per cubic meter ($/m 3), considering operational and financial aspects.([Shokri & Sanavi Fard, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0555)).

The present review presents a comprehensive analysis of the prevailing desalination technologies methodically. This text focuses on the research advancement of TD and, MBD and discusses their functioning principles. An analysis is conducted on the economic/cost benefits and environmental consequences of SD, as well as the evaluation of various SD methodologies, to predict the future possibilities of desalination technology. This review aims to establish a theoretical foundation for the choice and application of SD technology as a powerful weapon for the future.

Existing desalination technologies
----------------------------------

Desalination is a method for acquiring fresh water from seawater due to increased demand and decreased reserves. It divides saline input water into two streams, permeating fresh water with a TDS of less than 500 mg/L and rejecting brine-containing concentrated TDS. ([Elewa, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0165), [Sahu, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0530)).

### Technologies that rely on membranes

MBD technologies are technologies that do not involve phase transitions and differ from TD technologies in that they employ filtering saline water streams using selective removal of dissolved salts by semi-permeable membranes, rather than relying on temperature changes. The process of separating substances involves the use of external pressure, usually with the help of electrical energy, to counteract osmotic pressure and force water to move across a membrane while keeping the salts behind. The membrane technologies that have been installed on the largest scale include electrodialysis (ED) ([Naim et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0430)), reverse osmosis (RO) and nanofiltration (NF) ([Naim et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0435)). In contrast, other technologies like forward osmosis (FO) ([Mohammadifakhr et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0405)), pervaporation (PEV) ([Elsheniti et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0185)), membrane distillation (MD), ED reversal (EDR), ED metathesis (EDM) ([Chen et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0110)), liquid membrane (LM) ([Moneer et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0420)), membrane crystallisation (MCr) ([Ting et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0580)) and capacitive deionization (CDI) ([Elewa et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0170)) are promising technologies. These technologies have given rise to many variations that aim to enhance treatment efficiency and address a wide range of impacts ([Cipolletta et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0130), [Panagopoulos et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0465)). RO is an exceptional desalination method known for its minimal energy usage (3–5 kWh m−3) and ability to reclaim as much as 50% of freshwater ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#f0010)a). During this procedure, the application of pressure results in the passage of freshwater (known as permeate) through a partially permeable membrane, leading to the concentration of all salts in the brine that was rejected. Similar to other MBD, the primary challenges associated with this technique are membrane fouling issues and the frequent necessity of costly pre-treatments ([Charcosset, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0105)). Osmosis is the driving force behind FO, which is a naturally occurring phenomenon that provides the impression of being more energy-efficient, with energy usage ranging from 0.8 to 13 kWh m−3 and has the capability to recover up to 98% of freshwater ([Ahmad et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0015)). High-pressure RO (HPRO) is a cost-effective solution that requires minimal energy consumption (3–9 kWh m−3). It can be used for brine treatment salinity levels up to twice that of traditional RO systems (120–140 g/L TDS) by utilising increased pressure ([Subramani et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0570)). This is made possible by additional choices, such as osmotically assisted RO (OARO) ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#f0010)b), which allows for the sequential utilisation of multiple stages of RO and forward osmosis. When applied to the permeate side, as a sweep flow, OARO makes use of a salty stream that has a salinity that is either lower than or equal to that of the permeate. The osmotic pressure difference that exists between the membrane's two sides is decreased, as a result of this, which makes it possible for water to be transported even in situations when the feed's osmotic pressure is lower than the hydraulic pressure that comes from the outside ([Liang et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0370)). HPRO and OARO are viable substitutes for conventional RO. Increased water transport and reduced energy consumption are made possible by their potential to reduce the osmotic pressure gradient across the membrane. To achieve the effective advancement of these technologies, it is necessary to have robust and porous membranes that can endure elevated pressures and high levels of salt concentration. Several investigations have successfully produced membranes made of thin-film composite (TFC) that exhibit exceptional hydraulic burst resistance, reaching up to 110 bars. These membranes also demonstrate a remarkable NaCl rejection rate of over a high percentage of pure water and a 98 percent purity level of around 3 kg m-2 h−1 bar−1 ([Liang et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0370)). Ultimately, HPRO and OARO hold promise for enhancing energy efficiency and water retrieval in the desalination and treatment of briny waters. However, further investigation and advancement in the development and production of appropriate membranes for these procedures are necessary. To reduce the risk of membrane fouling, many membrane technologies need to undergo a pretreatment. Membrane fouling is the process of undesired chemicals building up within a membrane's pores or on its surface, resulting in decreased permeability and performance. The system's performance is influenced by factors such as the quality of feedwater, operating conditions, and the membrane's properties. Effective pretreatment techniques such as disinfection, filtration, flocculation, and coagulation are essential for minimizing membrane fouling in feedwater, decreasing solid particles, and enhancing efficiency and energy requirements. To ensure water purity and membrane protection, additional equipment, pretreatment devices, monitoring, and chemical application are needed. However, extensive pretreatment may introduce contaminants or generate waste streams ([Nthunya et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0445)). A potential alternative to exerting pressure in membrane technologies involves the application of direct current voltage to create a potential difference. Hence, ions with positive and negative charges move across specific membranes, with protons and anions migrating, respectively ([Peters & Hankins, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0480)). ED and its derivatives, including microbial desalination cells (MDC), EDM, and EDR are all examples of the kind of processes that exhibit these phenomena ([Prado de Nicolás, Molina-García, García-Bermejo, & Vera-García, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0495)). ED ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#f0010)c) efficiently reuses water, reduces waste, eliminates chemicals, and effectively removes non-ionic and ionic constituents like heavy metals, dyes, nutrients, and organic molecules. Depending on the kind of ion-exchange membranes (IEMs) that are used and the number of them, it is possible to attain TDS concentrations that are close to zero. Additionally, ED has minimal energy usage and operational expenses, rendering it a financially feasible choice for water treatment purposes. Nevertheless, it is important to take into account certain restrictions. ED is insufficient in eliminating pathogens, viruses, and bacteria, thus requiring other measures to guarantee the microbiological purity of water. Furthermore, the system encounters difficulties in treating extremely saline fluids due to the amplified electrical resistance and voltage loss, which might adversely impact its effectiveness. Finally, the widespread use of ED may be limited by the availability and expense of IEMs that have good selectivity, permeability, and chemical/mechanical stability ([Bello et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0065), [Morillo et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0425)). MD is an innovative membrane process that operates at different temperatures ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#f0010)d). It makes use of the variation in partial pressure on the two surfaces of a microporous membrane that is hydrophobic to facilitate the process by which molecules of water vapour are transferred from the input stream to the permeate ([Qtaishat & Banat, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0510)). There are multiple benefits associated with MD. Firstly, it enables the production of water with excellent permeability and high-water rejection. Like ED, MD does not necessitate the utilisation of chemicals and produces no deleterious by-products. It can effectively treat waters with high salinity levels (>70 g/L), which are difficult to treat using alternative methods like RO. Furthermore, MD demonstrates a notable advantage in terms of energy efficiency and operational expenses, as it has the capability to harness either waste heat or heat sources that are renewable ([Duong et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0150)). Avoiding membrane wetting and fouling, which can reduce process longevity and efficiency, requires proper pre-treatment. MD's effectiveness in eliminating volatile organic molecules is limited due to their ability to pollute membranes and the thermodynamic challenge of reaching TDS concentrations close to zero. The broad adoption of hydrophobic membranes with excellent permeability, porosity, and chemical and thermal stability could be limited by their availability and cost ([Julian et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0290)). To fully realise the promise of MD in different water treatment applications, it is imperative to tackle these problems. Membrane-based methods present benefits and drawbacks within the water treatment industry and desalination.

### Technologies that rely on heat as a primary mechanism

In TD systems, TDS is removed from saline water utilising external heat and energy, mimicking the natural water cycle, and resulting in freshwater formation. The thermal methods that have achieved the most commercial success are Multi-Effect Distillation (MED) and Multi-Stage Flash (MSF), ([Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#f0015)a). However, other technologies rely on brine concentration and crystallisation which are also becoming increasingly important ([Wei et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0615)).TD technologies, while generally competitive, are less lucrative and more energy-intensive than MBD solutions due to the considerable amount of energy they require and significant capital expenditure. Nevertheless, the prerequisites for pre-treatment are minor, and it is possible to treat brine with a wider range of TDS. In addition, TD methods are typically effective in treating small quantities of brine that have high levels of TDS and can achieve either minimal liquid discharge (MLD) or zero liquid discharge (ZLD). MSF and MED systems achieve a freshwater recovery of 85% while consuming 8–24 kWh m−3 of energy. These systems may treat influents with a TDS up to 180,000 mg/L ([Al-Sahali and Ettouney, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0035), [Subramani and Jacangelo, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0575)). One benefit of these arrangements is that multiple stages can be arranged sequentially to get a greater proportion of freshwater recovery. ### Themes

![Sustainable desalination: key to water security in the 21st century](https://smartwatermagazine.com/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail-830x455/public/desaladora-carboneras-50-28052024_0.jpg?itok=a_vlXVnc)

Desalination has **evolved from being a complementary technology to becoming a fundamental pillar of global water security**. With population growth, climate crises, and increasing water stress, more and more countries are turning to this solution to ensure the supply of drinking water and industrial use. Its development over the past decades has been remarkable, positioning the sector as a solid and continuously growing alternative. **Thanks to innovation and technological advancements, desalination is now more efficient, sustainable, and economically viable than ever**. Its implementation has not only guaranteed access to water in regions facing water stress but has also fostered the development of resilient infrastructures capable of addressing climate change scenarios. Moreover, desalination has proven to be a critical tool in mitigating the effects of prolonged droughts, offering a reliable supply source when traditional water reserves are depleted.

One of the most notable aspects is the **improvement in energy efficiency**. Modern desalination has significantly reduced its electricity consumption through energy recovery devices, high-performance motors, and artificial intelligence applied to process optimization. This progress has lowered operating costs and made the technology increasingly accessible. Additionally, new trends point toward greater integration with renewable energy sources, positioning desalination as a solution aligned with global sustainability and decarbonization goals. In this regard, technological innovation continues to play a key role, with advances in membrane optimization, reduced chemical consumption, and improvements in the efficiency of pretreatment and water distribution processes. Ongoing research in nanomaterials and graphene-based membranes promises even greater energy efficiency gains, potentially reducing desalination costs further and making the process even more environmentally friendly.

The environmental impact, once a major concern, is being mitigated through innovative strategies. **Brine management, once seen as a problem, is transforming into an opportunity** through the valorization of its components. Extracting minerals such as magnesium, lithium, and bromine is opening new possibilities, enabling desalination to contribute not only to water production but also to strategic sectors like energy and technology. Moreover, controlled discharge technologies and optimized brine dispersion strategies help minimize their impact on marine ecosystems, ensuring that desalination processes remain environmentally responsible. Additionally, some researchers are exploring ways to generate energy from brine, potentially transforming a by-product into a valuable resource.

The link between desalination and renewable energy is becoming stronger. Modern **desalination plants are increasingly relying on solar, wind, and green hydrogen as primary energy sources**. This model not only reduces the carbon footprint but also contributes to energy stability, ensuring a resilient and sustainable water supply. In Australia and Spain, implementing green energy purchase agreements and integrating solar farms into desalination plants have demonstrated that it is possible to operate with minimal environmental impact. The combination of these technologies, along with innovations in energy storage, is paving the way toward fully emissions-neutral desalination. Furthermore, the development of new hybrid systems that combine desalination with thermal energy storage or battery systems is allowing for optimized water production based on energy availability, maximizing process efficiency. As energy storage technology continues to advance, the reliance on fluctuating renewable sources will become even more manageable, making desalination plants increasingly independent from fossil fuel-based energy grids.

Digitalization is transforming the desalination sector by enhancing efficiency, reducing costs, and improving water quality. Modern plants utilize **remote monitoring, artificial intelligence, and digital twins to predict and prevent failures, ensuring stable operations**. Automation plays a crucial role in integrating desalination into regions with limited infrastructure, providing reliable water access with minimal staffing needs. AI-driven optimization is also reducing chemical reagent usage, improving both sustainability and cost efficiency. Additionally, blockchain-based water trading platforms are emerging, allowing utilities and industries to manage desalinated water distribution more effectively through smart contracts, further enhancing the sector’s economic viability.

The sector’s growth has driven new financing models, essential for its global expansion. **Public-private partnerships have been key in developing major infrastructure**, allowing businesses and governments to work together on desalination plant implementation. In regions like the Middle East, where desalination is critical, innovative financing schemes have enabled the rapid and efficient construction of megaprojects. Simultaneously, international financial institutions have started investing in green bonds and other instruments that facilitate investment in sustainable water infrastructure. This new financing landscape ensures that desalination can continue expanding without placing excessive financial burdens on consumers. Additionally, the development of water compensation mechanisms is enabling large corporations to contribute to desalination infrastructure financing as part of their sustainability strategies, generating benefits for both the industry and the environment. The emergence of private sector water utilities investing in desalination plants as part of diversified resource management strategies is also signaling a shift toward long-term commercial viability.

As technology advances and economies of scale make desalination more affordable, **an increasing number of countries are incorporating it into their water planning**. Spain has successfully consolidated a network of desalination plants that ensures supply in drought-prone regions, while Latin America and Africa are developing projects that improve water access in communities previously reliant on unreliable sources. In Asia, China is integrating desalination into its coastal development strategy, securing water resources for industry and its growing population. This global trend reinforces the idea that desalination is not only a viable solution but is becoming an essential component of sustainable water management worldwide. Engineers at MIT and in China are aiming to turn seawater into drinking water with a completely passive device that is inspired by the ocean, and powered by the sun.

In a paper appearing today in the journal *Joule,* the team outlines the design for a new solar desalination system that takes in saltwater and heats it with natural sunlight.

The configuration of the device allows water to circulate in swirling eddies, in a manner similar to the much larger “thermohaline” circulation of the ocean. This circulation, combined with the sun’s heat, drives water to evaporate, leaving salt behind. The resulting water vapor can then be condensed and collected as pure, drinkable water. In the meantime, the leftover salt continues to circulate through and out of the device, rather than accumulating and clogging the system.

The new system has a higher water-production rate and a higher salt-rejection rate than all other passive solar desalination concepts currently being tested.

The researchers estimate that if the system is scaled up to the size of a small suitcase, it could produce about 4 to 6 liters of drinking water per hour and last several years before requiring replacement parts. At this scale and performance, the system could produce drinking water at a rate and price that is cheaper than tap water.

“For the first time, it is possible for water, produced by sunlight, to be even cheaper than tap water,” says Lenan Zhang, a research scientist in MIT’s Device Research Laboratory.

The team envisions a scaled-up device could passively produce enough drinking water to meet the daily requirements of a small family. The system could also supply off-grid, coastal communities where seawater is easily accessible.

Zhang’s study co-authors include MIT graduate student Yang Zhong and Evelyn Wang, the Ford Professor of Engineering, along with Jintong Gao, Jinfang You, Zhanyu Ye, Ruzhu Wang, and Zhenyuan Xu of Shanghai Jiao Tong University in China.

**A powerful convection**

The team’s new system improves on their [previous design](https://news.mit.edu/2020/passive-solar-powered-water-desalination-0207) — a similar concept of multiple layers, called stages. Each stage contained an evaporator and a condenser that used heat from the sun to passively separate salt from incoming water. That design, which the team tested on the roof of an MIT building, efficiently converted the sun’s energy to evaporate water, which was then condensed into drinkable water. But the salt that was left over quickly accumulated as crystals that clogged the system after a few days. In a real-world setting, a user would have to place stages on a frequent basis, which would significantly increase the system’s overall cost.

In a follow-up effort, they [devised a solution](https://news.mit.edu/2022/solar-desalination-system-inexpensive-0214) with a similar layered configuration, this time with an added feature that helped to circulate the incoming water as well as any leftover salt. While this design prevented salt from settling and accumulating on the device, it desalinated water at a relatively low rate.

In the latest iteration, the team believes it has landed on a design that achieves both a high water-production rate, and high salt rejection, meaning that the system can quickly and reliably produce drinking water for an extended period. The key to their new design is a combination of their two previous concepts: a multistage system of evaporators and condensers, that is also configured to boost the circulation of water — and salt — within each stage.

“We introduce now an even more powerful convection, that is similar to what we typically see in the ocean, at kilometer-long scales,” Xu says.

The small circulations generated in the team’s new system is similar to the “thermohaline” convection in the ocean — a phenomenon that drives the movement of water around the world, based on differences in sea temperature (“thermo”) and salinity (“haline”).

“When seawater is exposed to air, sunlight drives water to evaporate. Once water leaves the surface, salt remains. And the higher the salt concentration, the denser the liquid, and this heavier water wants to flow downward,” Zhang explains. “By mimicking this kilometer-wide phenomena in small box, we can take advantage of this feature to reject salt.”

**Tapping out**

The heart of the team’s new design is a single stage that resembles a thin box, topped with a dark material that efficiently absorbs the heat of the sun. Inside, the box is separated into a top and bottom section. Water can flow through the top half, where the ceiling is lined with an evaporator layer that uses the sun’s heat to warm up and evaporate any water in direct contact. The water vapor is then funneled to the bottom half of the box, where a condensing layer air-cools the vapor into salt-free, drinkable liquid. The researchers set the entire box at a tilt within a larger, empty vessel, then attached a tube from the top half of the box down through the bottom of the vessel, and floated the vessel in saltwater.

In this configuration, water can naturally push up through the tube and into the box, where the tilt of the box, combined with the thermal energy from the sun, induces the water to swirl as it flows through. The small eddies help to bring water in contact with the upper evaporating layer while keeping salt circulating, rather than settling and clogging.

The team built several prototypes, with one, three, and 10 stages, and tested their performance in water of varying salinity, including natural seawater and water that was seven times saltier.

From these tests, the researchers calculated that if each stage were scaled up to a square meter, it would produce up to 5 liters of drinking water per hour, and that the system could desalinate water without accumulating salt for several years. Given this extended lifetime, and the fact that the system is entirely passive, requiring no electricity to run, the team estimates that the overall cost of running the system would be cheaper than what it costs to produce tap water in the United States.

“We show that this device is capable of achieving a long lifetime,” Zhong says. “That means that, for the first time, it is possible for drinking water produced by sunlight to be cheaper than tap water. This opens up the possibility for solar desalination to address real-world problems.”

“This is a very innovative approach that effectively mitigates key challenges in the field of desalination,” says Guihua Yu, who develops sustainable water and energy storage systems at the University of Texas at Austin, and was not involved in the research. “The design is particularly beneficial for regions struggling with high-salinity water. Its modular design makes it highly suitable for household water production, allowing for scalability and adaptability to meet individual needs.”

Funding for the research at Shanghai Jiao Tong University was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Sustainable desalination reduces environmental impacts, conserves energy, and utilizes renewable resources, ensuring long-term water supply while minimizing ecological damage and fostering resilience against climate change.

enriched_points.point_title: Role of technology in future solutions
enriched_points.expanded_content: Technological advancements, such as energy recovery devices and innovative materials, enhance efficiency, lower costs, and integrate renewable energy sources, paving the way for sustainable desalination systems.

Cooperation between governments, private sectors, and communities is crucial for funding, developing infrastructure, and implementing sustainable practices, ensuring equitable access to freshwater resources globally.
